titleStmt.title: SII 1.59: original edition by Eugen Hultzsch
PART II. TAMIL AND GRANTHA INSCRIPTIONS. III. INSCRIPTIONS AT AND NEAR VIRI√ëCHIPURAM. No. 59. ON THE BASE OF THE NORTH WALL

summary.p: This inscription is dated in the twenty-second year of Tribhuvanachakravartin RƒÅjarƒÅja-deva and in the ≈öaka year 1160. It records the gift of the village of KumƒÅrama·πÖgalam, which was situated east of Ko·πü·πüa-ma·πÖgalam, north-west of Aimb≈´·πá·∏çi‚Äî which lay to the north of Poygai, alias RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a-nall≈´r‚Äîand south of the PƒÅla·πüu. Aimb≈´·πá·∏çi is the old name of the modern village of Ammu·πá·∏çi; it occurs also in an Ammu·πá·∏çi inscription, which will be noticed in Part III (No. 131). The PƒÅlƒÅ·πüu is the well-known PƒÅlƒÅr, the chief river of the North Arcot District.

revisionDesc.change: Further conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)
Conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)

div.p:
svasti ≈õrƒ´ . cakaraiyƒÅ·πá·π≠u ƒÄyirattoru·πâ≈´·πü·πüa·πüupatu cellƒÅ·πâi·πâ·πüa ≈õrƒ´tiripuva·πâaccakkaravattika·∏∑ ≈õrƒ´rƒÅjarƒÅjadeva·πüku yƒÅ
·πá·π≠u Irupattira·πá·π≠ƒÅvatu tai poykai ƒÄ·πâa IrƒÅcentiraco·∏ªanall≈´rccittirame·∏ªimalaima·πá·π≠alavi·πá·πáakarƒÅ
·πâa Aru·∏∑ƒÅ·∏∑apperumƒÅ·∏∑ukkuttiruvi·π≠aiyƒÅ·π≠·π≠amƒÅkakkumƒÅrama·πÖkalam . melpƒÅ·πükellai ko·πü·πüama·πÖkalattu Ellai ƒÄca·πüutiyi·πâa·π≠·π≠a tiruvƒÅ·∏ªikkallukku ki·∏ªak
kum . te·πâpƒÅ·πükellai poykaiyƒÅ·πâa IrƒÅcentiraco·∏ªanall≈´rkku va·π≠akkƒÅ·πâa Aimp≈´·πá·π≠i kayakkƒÅlukku va·π≠akkum . ki·∏ªpƒÅ·πükellai Aimp≈´·πá·π≠i Ellai ƒÄca·πüutiyil na·π≠·π≠a ti
ruvƒÅ·∏ªikkallukku me·πükkum . va·π≠apƒÅ·πükkellai pƒÅlƒÅ·πü·πüukku te·πükku minta ·πâƒÅ·πüppƒÅ·πükkellaikkumu·π≠pa·π≠·π≠a me·πâokki·πâa maramum ki·πáokki·πâa ki·πáa·πüum na√±cai pu√±cai ka·π≠amai ku·π≠i‚åà
mai U·∏∑pa·π≠a ve·π≠·π≠ita·πâiƒÄ·∏∑ ƒÄyam pƒÅ·π≠ikƒÅval cilvari peruvari kƒÅ·πütikaiArici kƒÅ·πütikaippaccai ma·πü·πüumu·∏∑·∏∑a pala kƒÅcƒÅyamka·∏∑um ta·πüiI·πüai ka·π≠aiI·πüai ta·π≠·π≠ƒÅrppƒÅ·π≠·π≠a√±ce
kkukka·π≠amaiyum ƒÄcivakakƒÅcu Eppe·πüpa·π≠·π≠a A·πâaittƒÅya·πÖka·∏∑um ƒÄyaƒ™·πâƒÅr ci·πüai IrƒÅma·πâ kera·∏∑a·πâ pakkal po·πâ·πâa·πüakko·πá·π≠u ma·πá·πáa·πüappoykai Aru·∏∑ƒÅ·∏∑apperumƒÅ·∏∑ukkut
tiruvi·π≠aiyƒÅ·π≠·π≠amƒÅka cantairƒÅtittavarai celvatƒÅka vi·π≠·π≠e·πâ ce·πÖke·πái virƒÅca·πâi Ammaiyappa·πâ ta·πâi·πâi·πâ·πüu ve·πâ·πüƒÅ·πâ ta·πâ vaci kƒÅ·π≠·π≠uvƒÅ·πâ A·∏ªakiya co·∏ªa·πâƒÅ·πâa Etirili

Hail! Prosperity! [In the month of] Tai of the twenty-second year of the illustrious Tribhuvanachakravartin, the illustrious RƒÅjarƒÅja-deva, which was current during the ≈öaka year one thousand one hundred and sixty,‚ÄîI, ≈öe·πÖge·πái-Vƒ´rƒÅ≈õani-Ammaiyappa·πâ, who has gained victory standing by himself, who shows his sword, A·πõagiya-≈öo·πõa·πâ, alias Edirili . . . . . . . . . . , after having received gold from RƒÅma the Kera·∏∑a, a slave (i.e., worshipper) of ƒÄya-ƒ´·πâƒÅr,This is probably a name of Vish·πáu. It might mean: ‚Äúhe who is without a mother,‚Äù and correspond to the Sanskrit Aja, ‚Äúthe unborn.‚Äù Or could it be a corruption of aiya·πâƒÅr, a forest deity of the Tamil districts?‚Äîgave to the Vish·πáu temple of Chitra-me·πõi-Malaima·πá·∏çala, alias (the temple of) Aru·∏∑ƒÅ·∏∑a-PerumƒÅ·∏∑, (at) Poygai, alias RƒÅjendra-≈öo·πõanall≈´r, (the village of) KumƒÅra-ma·πÖgalam as exclusive property,See page 69, note 2. to last as long as the moon and the sun;‚Äîthe boundary on the western side is to the east of the tiruvƒÅ·πõiAccording to the Dictionnaive Tamoul-Fran√ßais, tiruvƒÅ·∏ªi means ‚Äúa royal seal.‚Äù stone put up at the extremity of the boundary of Ko·πü·πüa-ma·πÖgalam; the boundary on the southern side is to the north of the channel of Aimb≈´·πá·∏çi, which lies to the north of Poygai, alias RƒÅjendra-≈öo·πõa-nall≈´r; the boundary on the eastern side is to the west of the tiruvƒÅ·πõi stone put up at the extremity of the boundary of Aimb≈´·πá·∏çi; the boundary on the northern side is to the south of the (river) PƒÅlƒÅ·πüu;‚Äîthe trees overground and the wells underground, the wet land and the dry land, included within these boundaries in the four directions; including taxes and rights; (the revenue for) one Ve·π≠·π≠i,See page 82, note 3. tolls (ƒÅyam), the small taxes (and) the large taxes for the village-police, the rice in KƒÅrttika, the unripe (fruit) in KƒÅrttika, and all other revenue in money; the tax on looms, the tax on shops, the tax on goldsmiths, the tax on oil-mills, the tax on ƒÄjƒ´vakas,The ƒÄjƒ´vakas are the Jainas In stead of ƒÄcivakakƒÅcu we read ƒÄcuvakaka·π≠amai in three other Poygai inscriptions and ƒÄcuvika·πâ perkka·π≠amai in the Pa·∏çave·∏çu inscription No. 78. and all (other) revenue.
Digital edition of SII 1.59 by converted to DHARMA conventions by Emmanuel Francis.
